title: Treating active woodworm in furniture
subtitle: Apply permethrin woodworm fluid to exit holes in timber to stop an active infestation
excerpt: How to identify and treat an active woodworm infestation in furniture or structural timbers, covering testing for activity, applying permethrin-based woodworm fluid by injection and brush, and sealing the timber after treatment.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Household Guide (furniture). Collins Complete DIY Manual. The Workshop Companion (1913).

glossaryTerms.definition: The 1 to 2 mm round hole left in timber by an adult wood-boring beetle as it emerges from the wood. The beetle develops as a larva inside the wood for one to five years before pupating and boring out. Fresh flight holes are clean-edged and surrounded by a light-coloured bore dust called frass. Old holes are darkened and have no frass.

A woodworm infestation where larvae are still present and feeding inside the timber. Signs of active infestation: fresh flight holes (clean, pale inside, visible frass on the surface), creaking or movement in structural timbers beyond what is expected, and new holes appearing each year between May and October, when adult beetles emerge. Old holes with no frass and dark, worn interiors indicate a past infestation that may have ended without treatment.

content.text: Most woodworm in domestic furniture is the common furniture beetle (Anobium punctatum). The
are 1 to 2 mm across. Confirm the infestation is
before treating: old holes in an antique piece may represent a past infestation that ended decades ago. Fresh frass (bore dust) around or below the holes is the clearest sign of current activity.

Permethrin-based woodworm fluid (Cuprinol Woodworm Killer or equivalent). A 25 mm brush and a syringe or injector for applying fluid into the holes. Rubber gloves and eye protection. Good ventilation: the spirit carrier in woodworm fluid is flammable and gives off strong fumes. Lay the dust sheet under the furniture to catch any drips.
Step 1, confirm the infestation is active

Look for fresh flight holes: they are clean, pale, and round, with frass around or below them. Old holes are darker inside and have no fresh frass. Inspect the flight holes between May and October when beetles are active: new holes appear in this window. Seal all visible holes with a strip of masking tape in early spring and check in September for new holes punched through the tape: this confirms ongoing activity.
Step 2, move the furniture outdoors or to a ventilated area

Move the item to a well-ventilated area or outdoors on a dry day. Remove any drawers and treat them separately. Remove any upholstery that cannot be wetted: woodworm fluid will stain fabric. Wear rubber gloves throughout. Keep children and pets out of the treatment area until the fluid has fully dried.

Brush the woodworm fluid liberally over all bare timber surfaces, working it into the grain. Apply two coats to the end grain where the fluid penetrates most easily to the internal galleries. Use the syringe or injector to squirt fluid directly into each flight hole: the fluid is drawn into the galleries by capillary action. Apply until each hole is full and the surrounding timber is wet. Allow the first coat to soak in for 10 minutes, then apply a second coat.
Step 4, allow to dry and seal the timber

Allow the treated timber to dry fully before bringing it back inside (24 to 48 hours, depending on the weather and the number of coats). Once fully dry, any existing wax or polish finish can be reapplied. For bare structural timbers in a loft or under floor, no sealing is needed: leave the treated timber bare so it can breathe. The permethrin remains active in the timber for many years.

Death-watch beetle attacks old hardwood timbers in historic buildings. It makes a larger 3 mm flight hole. Treat it with the same permethrin fluid, but the galleries run deeper and the timber often needs a second treatment the following year. House longhorn beetle must be reported to the local authority in some UK counties and needs a specialist to treat it, not DIY fluid.

Check treated furniture annually for new flight holes for two to three years after treatment. A single treatment usually stops an active infestation, but untreated neighbouring timber can re-introduce larvae. In a loft, treat all structural timbers in the same session: a patchy treatment leaves untreated timber as a source of re-infestation.

attrs.symptom: New holes appear in the season after treatment
attrs.cause: Larvae that were deep in the galleries at the time of treatment survived the first application and completed their development.
attrs.fix: Apply a second treatment. Two applications in consecutive years cover the full larval development cycle for most species. Check that all accessible surfaces were treated in the first session.

attrs.symptom: Timber is structurally weak or crumbling after treating
attrs.cause: The infestation has been active for many years and the internal gallery network has reduced the timber to a hollow shell.
attrs.fix: Structural timbers with more than 30% of their cross-section consumed by galleries must be replaced, not just treated. Consolidate furniture timber with a penetrating hardener (acrylic or epoxy consolidant) before applying the woodworm fluid.
